abstract: This paper reports on empirical and practical experiences using large language models (LLMs) for text classification and annotation tasks in communication studies. Drawing on collaborative work across multiple research teams, it examines both the transformative potential of these tools and the emerging methodological challenges they introduce. No abstract was provided, so further detail cannot be summarized faithfully.
key_claims: LLMs are transformative tools for text classification and annotation in communication studies
Their adoption introduces emerging methodological and practical challenges that the field must address
methods: Synthesis of empirical and practical experiences from multiple research teams applying LLMs to text classification and annotation tasks (specific methods not stated in available material)
findings: Specific findings are not available without access to the full paper or abstract
contributions: Provides a collective account of how LLMs are being applied to communication research tasks
Surfaces practical challenges and considerations relevant to researchers adopting LLMs for annotation and classification
framing: The paper is positioned within communication studies methodology, specifically the ongoing conversation about computational and automated text analysis and the integration of generative AI tools into established annotation and classification workflows.
